docs.title: Build customer support agents as code.
docs.text:
Cognidesk is a TypeScript runtime SDK that gives you full control over conversations, state machines, tools, knowledge retrieval, and UI — without vendor lock-in.

  • Get started in 5 minutes

    Install Cognidesk and create your first agent with tools and journeys.

    Getting Started

  • Understand the concepts

    Learn the domain model: Agents, Journeys, Tools, Knowledge Sources, and Events.

    Concepts

  • Follow the guides

    Step-by-step walkthroughs for every SDK capability.

    Guides

  • API Reference

    Full type signatures and exports for every package.

    API Reference

docs.title: Quick example
docs.text:
import { createAgent, createRuntime, tool } from "@cognidesk/core";
import { createSqliteStorage } from "@cognidesk/storage/sqlite";
import { z } from "zod";

const findTicket = tool("findTicket", {
  input: z.object({ bookingReference: z.string() }),
  output: z.object({ status: z.string() }),
  execute: async ({ input }) => ({
    status: `Ticket ${input.bookingReference} is confirmed.`,
  }),
});

const agent = createAgent("support", {
  instructions: "You are a helpful support agent.",
})
  .tools.add(findTicket)
  .compile();

const runtime = createRuntime({
  storage: createSqliteStorage({ filename: "data.sqlite" }),
  agent,
  models, // Your model configuration
});

docs.title: Why Cognidesk?
docs.text: Feature Cognidesk Typical AI frameworks State machine journeys  Built-in DSL  DIY or none Transport neutral  Core has zero HTTP deps  Framework-bound Typed tools & knowledge  Zod schemas everywhere  Loose typing Voice support  First-class adapter  Separate product React UI kit  Included  Build your own OpenTelemetry  Native spans & metrics  Manual instrumentation

docs.text:
Complete type signatures and exports for every Cognidesk package.

Hybrid documentation

Each package reference includes auto-generated type signatures alongside hand-written usage examples. Type signatures are generated from the TypeScript source using TypeDoc.

docs.text: Package Description @cognidesk/core Runtime, agents, journeys, tools, knowledge, events @cognidesk/http HTTP + SSE transport adapter @cognidesk/model Model provider adapters @cognidesk/react React hooks and chat widget @cognidesk/ui Prebuilt UI components @cognidesk/storage Storage adapters @cognidesk/otel OpenTelemetry instrumentation @cognidesk/voice-openai OpenAI Realtime voice adapter @cognidesk/voice-websocket Voice WebSocket adapter @cognidesk/journey-index-json JSON-based journey index

docs.title: @cognidesk/core
docs.text: The core package contains the runtime, agent builder, journey DSL, tool and knowledge definitions, and event system.

docs.title: Main exports
docs.text:
import {
  createAgent,
  createRuntime,
  tool,
  knowledgeSource,
  customRuntimeEvent,
  buildJourneyIndex,
} from "@cognidesk/core";

docs.title: createAgent(id, options)
docs.text:
Creates an agent builder instance.

const builder = createAgent("flight-support", {
  instructions: "You are a concise flight support agent.",
});

Parameters:

Parameter Type Description id string Unique agent identifier options.instructions string System prompt for the agent

Returns: AgentBuilder

docs.title: createRuntime(options)
docs.text:
Creates the conversation runtime.

const runtime = createRuntime({ storage, agent, models });
await runtime.initialize();

Parameters:

Parameter Type Description options.storage Storage Storage adapter options.agent Agent Compiled agent options.models ModelSet Model configuration options.journeyIndex JourneyIndex Optional journey index options.streaming StreamingOptions Optional streaming config

Returns: Runtime

docs.title: tool(name, options)
docs.text:
Defines a typed tool.

const findTicket = tool("findTicket", {
  input: z.object({ bookingReference: z.string() }),
  output: z.object({ status: z.string() }),
  execute: async ({ input }) => ({ status: "confirmed" }),
});

docs.text:
type RuntimeEvent = 
  | RuntimeEventBase<"message.started", {
  role: "assistant" | "user";
}>
  | RuntimeEventBase<"message.delta", {
  textDelta: string;
}>
  | RuntimeEventBase<"message.completed", {
  intermediate?: boolean;
  segments?: MessageSegment[];
  text: string;
  usage?: UsageRecord;
  visibleToModel?: boolean;
}>
  | RuntimeEventBase<"message.aborted", {
  partialText?: string;
  reason: string;
}>
  | RuntimeEventBase<"voice.segment.started", {
  adapter: string;
  channelSegmentId: string;
  connectionId: string;
  provider?: string;
}>
  | RuntimeEventBase<"voice.segment.ended", {
  channelSegmentId: string;
  connectionId?: string;
  reason?: string;
}>

  message?: string;

  | RuntimeEventBase<"journey.event.emitted", {

  };
}>
  | RuntimeEventBase<"journey.state.entered", {
  journeyId: string;
  stateId: string;
}>
  | RuntimeEventBase<"journey.extraction.proposed", {
  fields: string[];
  journeyId: string;
  stateId: string;
}>
  | RuntimeEventBase<"journey.extraction.accepted", {
  fields: string[];
  journeyId: string;
  stateId: string;
}>
  | RuntimeEventBase<"action.started", {
  actionName: string;
  journeyId?: string;
  stateId?: string;
}>
  | RuntimeEventBase<"action.completed", {
  actionName: string;
  error?: string;
  journeyId?: string;
  stateId?: string;
  success: boolean;
}>
  | RuntimeEventBase<"tool.started", {

}>

docs.title: RuntimeEventInput
docs.text:
type RuntimeEventInput<TType> = Omit<Extract<RuntimeEvent, {
  type: TType;
}>, "id" | "offset" | "createdAt" | "telemetry"> & {
  createdAt?: string;
  id?: string;
  telemetry?: RuntimeEvent["telemetry"];
};

docs.title: ToolRunOptionsFor
docs.text:
type ToolRunOptionsFor<TTool, TContext> = TTool extends SideEffectTool ? ToolRunOptions<TTool, TContext> & {
  confirm: ConfirmationPolicy;
} : ToolRunOptions<TTool, TContext> & {
  confirm?: ConfirmationPolicy;
};

The adapter implements a structured JSON protocol over WebSocket:

  • Voice Audio Deltas (16-bit PCM, mono, 24 kHz)
  • Session control events (start, stop, interrupt)
  • Status and error events
  • Transcript notifications

docs.title: Architecture decisions
docs.text:
This project uses Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) to document significant technical decisions. You can find them in docs/adr/.

The domain language and terminology are defined in CONTEXT.md. Please use the established vocabulary when contributing code or documentation.

docs.title: Pull requests
docs.text:
1. Create a feature branch from main
  2. Make your changes
  3. Ensure pnpm check passes
  4. Submit a pull request with a clear description

docs.title: Code style
docs.text:
• TypeScript strict mode
  • Use the established domain language from CONTEXT.md
  • Keep functions focused and well-typed
  • Add TSDoc comments for public API exports

docs.title: Reporting bugs
docs.text:
Open an issue with:

  • Steps to reproduce
  • Expected behavior
  • Actual behavior
  • Environment (Node version, OS, package versions)

docs.title: Concepts
docs.text: Cognidesk has a precise domain language. Understanding these concepts will make the guides and API reference much clearer.

docs.title: Core building blocks
docs.text: Concept What it is Agent A compiled definition that owns instructions, tools, knowledge, and journeys Journey A state machine that guides a conversation through structured steps Tool A typed function the agent can invoke during a conversation Knowledge Source A retrieval-augmented source the agent can query for context Runtime Event A typed event emitted during conversation execution Transport Neutrality The principle that core has zero transport dependencies

docs.title: How they fit together
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Agent Definition] --> B[Runtime]
    B --> C[Conversation]
    C --> D[Journey Activation]
    D --> E[State Transitions]
    E --> F[Tool Execution]
    E --> G[Knowledge Retrieval]
    B --> H[Runtime Events]
    B --> I[HTTP Adapter]
    B --> J[Voice Adapter]

The Runtime is the execution engine. It takes a compiled Agent definition and manages Conversations. During a conversation, the runtime may activate a Journey (a state machine), execute Tools, retrieve Knowledge, and emit Runtime Events.

Adapters (HTTP, Voice, Storage) connect the transport-neutral runtime to the outside world.

docs.title: Agents
docs.text: An Agent is the top-level definition in Cognidesk. It encapsulates everything the AI needs to operate: instructions, tools, knowledge sources, journeys, and custom events.

docs.title: Definition vs. compilation
docs.text:
Cognidesk separates agent definition (builder pattern) from agent compilation (frozen, validated artifact):

// Definition phase — mutable
const builder = createAgent("support", {
  instructions: "You are a helpful support agent.",
});
builder.tools.add(findTicket);
builder.knowledge.add(faq);

// Compilation — immutable, validated
const agent = builder.compile();

Why compile?

Compilation validates that all journeys, tools, and knowledge sources are correctly wired. It catches configuration errors at startup rather than at runtime.

docs.title: Agent identity
docs.text:
Each agent has a unique string id used to:

  • Route conversations to the correct agent
  • Scope storage (conversations belong to an agent)
  • Identify the agent in telemetry and events

docs.title: Instructions
docs.text:
Instructions are the system prompt for the agent. They define tone, behavior, and constraints:

const agent = createAgent("support", {
  instructions: `You are a concise customer support agent for FlightCo.
Rules:
- Never invent flight information
- Always ask for a booking reference before looking up tickets
- Be empathetic but brief`,
});

docs.title: What belongs on an agent
docs.text: Capability Method Purpose Tools builder.tools.add(...) Actions the agent can take Knowledge builder.knowledge.add(...) Context the agent can retrieve Journeys builder.stateMachineJourney(...) Structured conversation flows Custom Events builder.customEvents.add(...) App-layer event types

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Tools — define typed actions
  • Journeys — add structured flows
  • Knowledge Sources — provide retrieval context

docs.title: Journeys
docs.text: A Journey is a state machine that guides a conversation through structured steps. Journeys are the core differentiator of Cognidesk — they give you deterministic control over conversation flow while still leveraging LLM flexibility.

docs.title: Why state machines?
docs.text:
Most AI frameworks let the model decide what to do next. This works for simple Q&A but breaks down for structured workflows:

  • Collecting required information in a specific order
  • Enforcing business rules between steps
  • Handling conditional paths (auth required, feature flags)
  • Providing deterministic audit trails

Cognidesk journeys solve this by defining states and transitions that the runtime enforces.

docs.title: Anatomy of a journey
docs.text:
const ticketStatus = agentBuilder.stateMachineJourney("ticket-status", {
  condition: "Customer wants ticket status", // (1)!
  examples: ["Can you check ticket ABC123?"], // (2)!
  context: z.object({ // (3)!
    bookingReference: z.string().optional(),
    ticketStatus: z.string().optional(),
  }),
});
  1. Natural language condition for journey activation
  2. Example utterances that trigger this journey
  3. Typed context that persists across states

docs.title: States and transitions
docs.text:
const states = ticketStatus.defineStates("identify", "lookup");

const identify = states.get("identify").collect("bookingReference");
const lookup = states.get("lookup").runTool(findTicket, {
  input: ({ context }) => ({ bookingReference: context.bookingReference ?? "" }),
  assign: { ticketStatus: ({ output }) => output.status },
});

ticketStatus.initial(identify);
identify.transitionTo(lookup);

docs.title: Guards
docs.text:
Guards conditionally allow or block journey activation:

const secured = agentBuilder.stateMachineJourney("secured-review", {
  condition: "Customer needs secured review",
  context: z.object({}),
  guard: ({ app }) =>
    app.authenticated
      ? { allow: true }
      : { allow: false, code: "auth_required", message: "Please authenticate first." },
});

docs.title: Context reuse
docs.text:
Journeys can reuse context from previous journey instances:

const status = agentBuilder.stateMachineJourney("ticket-status", {
  // ...
  contextReuse: {
    fields: ["bookingReference"],
    when: ({ previousContext, turn }) =>
      previousContext.bookingReference !== undefined && turn.reusePreviousTicket === true,
  },
});

docs.title: Journey Events
docs.text:
Journeys can declare typed events for app-driven state changes:

const ticketSynced = ticketStatus.event("ticket.synced", {
  payload: z.object({ bookingReference: z.string() }),
  routing: "activeJourneyOnly",
});

lookup.on(ticketSynced).target(lookup);

docs.title: Key principles
docs.text:
Design principles

  • Each journey owns its own typed context
  • States are explicit — no hidden transitions
  • Guards run before activation, not during
  • Journey events enable external systems to drive state changes

docs.title: Knowledge Sources
docs.text: A Knowledge Source is a retrieval-augmented context provider. Knowledge sources let the agent query external information during a conversation without baking everything into the system prompt.

docs.title: Defining a knowledge source
docs.text:
import { knowledgeSource } from "@cognidesk/core";
import { z } from "zod";

const faq = knowledgeSource("support-faq", {
  query: z.object({ query: z.string() }),
  metadata: z.object({ source: z.string(), lastUpdated: z.string().optional() }),
  retrieve: async ({ query }) => ({
    items: [
      {
        id: "refund-policy",
        content: "Refunds are available within 24 hours of booking.",
        metadata: { source: "faq", lastUpdated: "2024-01-15" },
      },
    ],
  }),
});

docs.title: How retrieval works
docs.text:
1. The runtime determines that knowledge is needed (via journey state or model request)
  2. The knowledge source's retrieve function is called with a typed query
  3. Results are injected into the conversation context
  4. The model uses the retrieved content to inform its response

docs.title: Typed metadata
docs.text:
Each knowledge item carries typed metadata. This enables:

  • Source attribution in UI (hoverable citations)
  • Filtering and ranking in retrieval
  • Audit trails for compliance

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Tip

  • Keep retrieval functions fast — they run in the hot path
  • Return only relevant items, not entire knowledge bases
  • Use metadata for source attribution in the UI
  • Consider caching for frequently-accessed sources

docs.title: Runtime Events
docs.text: Runtime Events are typed events emitted during conversation execution. They form the observable contract between the runtime and external systems (UIs, analytics, webhooks).

docs.title: Event categories
docs.text:
Cognidesk emits several categories of events:

Category Examples Purpose Conversation message, turn complete Core conversation flow Journey activated, state changed, completed Journey lifecycle Tool invoked, completed, failed Tool execution Knowledge retrieved, injected Knowledge retrieval Custom Developer-defined App-specific events

docs.title: Custom events
docs.text:
Define app-specific events with typed payloads:

import { customRuntimeEvent } from "@cognidesk/core";
import { z } from "zod";

const leadCaptured = customRuntimeEvent("lead.captured", {
  payload: z.object({
    email: z.string().email(),
    source: z.string(),
  }),
  visibleToModel: true, // (1)!
});
  1. When true, the event payload is included in the model's context.

docs.title: Emitting custom events
docs.text:
await runtime.emitCustomEvent({
  conversationId: conversation.id,
  event: leadCaptured,
  payload: { email: "customer@example.com", source: "pricing-page" },
});

docs.title: Consuming events
docs.text:
Events are available via:

  • SSE streaming — real-time push to clients
  • Event history — query past events for a conversation
  • Event replay — replay events for debugging or recovery
  • OpenTelemetry — events as span events in traces

docs.title: Design principles
docs.text:
Info

  • Events are immutable once emitted
  • Every event has a timestamp and conversation scope
  • Custom events extend the built-in event vocabulary
  • visibleToModel controls whether the model sees the event in context

docs.title: Tools
docs.text: A Tool is a typed function that the agent can invoke during a conversation. Tools are how agents take actions — looking up data, calling APIs, or performing operations.

docs.title: Defining a tool
docs.text:
Every tool has a name, typed input/output schemas (using Zod), and an execute function:

import { tool } from "@cognidesk/core";
import { z } from "zod";

const findTicket = tool("findTicket", {
  input: z.object({
    bookingReference: z.string().describe("The booking reference to look up"),
  }),
  output: z.object({
    status: z.string(),
    departure: z.string().optional(),
  }),
  execute: async ({ input }) => {
    const ticket = await db.findTicket(input.bookingReference);
    return { status: ticket.status, departure: ticket.departure };
  },
});

docs.title: Type safety
docs.text:
Tools are fully typed end-to-end:

  • Input: Validated before execution via Zod schema
  • Output: Validated after execution
  • Context: Available in the execute function

The model sees the schema as a function signature and knows exactly what arguments to provide.

docs.title: Tools in journeys
docs.text:
Tools can be invoked as part of a journey state transition:

const lookup = states.get("lookup").runTool(findTicket, {
  input: ({ context }) => ({ bookingReference: context.bookingReference ?? "" }),
  assign: { ticketStatus: ({ output }) => output.status },
});

This provides deterministic tool execution — the runtime calls the tool at the right point in the flow rather than relying on the model to decide.

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Tip

  • Keep tools focused on a single responsibility
  • Use .describe() on Zod fields to help the model understand parameters
  • Validate and sanitize inputs in the execute function
  • Return structured data, not natural language

docs.title: Transport Neutrality
docs.text: Transport Neutrality is the architectural principle that the Cognidesk runtime has zero dependencies on HTTP, WebSockets, or any specific transport protocol.

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text:
Many AI frameworks bind the runtime directly to an HTTP server. This creates problems:

  • Can't run in serverless functions without adaptation
  • Can't embed in CLI tools or desktop apps
  • Can't test without standing up a server
  • Can't swap transports (HTTP → WebSocket → gRPC)

Cognidesk's core package (@cognidesk/core) imports no HTTP libraries, no server frameworks, and no streaming protocols.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
graph LR
    A["@cognidesk/core"] --> B["Pure runtime logic"]
    C["@cognidesk/http"] --> A
    D["@cognidesk/voice-websocket"] --> A
    E["Your custom transport"] --> A

The runtime exposes a programmatic API. Transport adapters (HTTP, Voice, custom) wrap that API for specific protocols:

// Core — no transport awareness
const runtime = createRuntime({ storage, agent, models });
const response = await runtime.sendMessage({ conversationId, message });

// HTTP adapter — wraps core for REST + SSE
const handler = createCognideskHttpHandler({ runtime, agentId, basePath: "/api" });

// Voice adapter — wraps core for realtime audio
const voiceHandler = createVoiceHandler({ runtime, agentId });

docs.title: What this enables
docs.text: Deployment Transport Works with core? Express/Fastify server HTTP + SSE Cloudflare Workers HTTP CLI tool Direct function calls Desktop app (Electron) IPC Test suite In-memory Voice call WebSocket

docs.title: Built-in adapters
docs.text:
Cognidesk ships adapters for common transports as separate packages:

  • @cognidesk/http — REST POST + SSE streaming
  • @cognidesk/voice-websocket — Voice browser protocol
  • @cognidesk/voice-openai — OpenAI Realtime connection

These are optional conveniences — you can always build your own adapter against the core API.

docs.text:
Get up and running with Cognidesk in minutes. This section covers installation, basic setup, and building your first agent.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Node.js 18+ or compatible runtime (Bun, Deno)
  • TypeScript 5.0+ (recommended)
  • pnpm, npm, or yarn
  • An LLM provider account (OpenAI, OpenRouter, or compatible)

docs.title: Add optional packages
docs.text:
Cognidesk is modular. Install only what you need:

Package Purpose @cognidesk/core Runtime, agents, journeys, tools, knowledge @cognidesk/http HTTP/SSE transport adapter @cognidesk/model Model provider adapters @cognidesk/react React hooks and chat widget @cognidesk/ui Prebuilt UI components @cognidesk/storage Storage adapters @cognidesk/otel OpenTelemetry instrumentation @cognidesk/voice-openai Voice via OpenAI Realtime @cognidesk/voice-websocket Voice WebSocket adapter @cognidesk/journey-index-json JSON-based journey index

docs.title: TypeScript configuration
docs.text:
Cognidesk requires strict mode and ES2022+ target:

tsconfig.json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "strict": true,
    "target": "ES2022",
    "module": "ESNext",
    "moduleResolution": "bundler"
  }
}

docs.title: Agents & Instructions
docs.text:
This guide covers agent configuration, instruction design, and compilation.

Work in progress

This guide is being written. Check back soon for complete content.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Agents are the top-level entry point to Cognidesk. An agent definition encapsulates:

  • Instructions — the system prompt that shapes behavior
  • Tools — actions the agent can take
  • Knowledge sources — context the agent can retrieve
  • Journeys — structured conversation flows
  • Custom events — app-specific event types

docs.title: Creating an agent
docs.text:
import { createAgent } from "@cognidesk/core";

const builder = createAgent("flight-support", {
  instructions: `You are a concise flight support agent for FlightCo.

Rules:
- Never invent flight information
- Always ask for a booking reference before looking up tickets
- Be empathetic but brief
- If you cannot help, offer to connect the customer with a human agent`,
});

docs.title: Instruction design tips
docs.text:
Effective instructions

  • Start with a role statement ("You are...")
  • List explicit rules as bullet points
  • Define boundaries (what the agent should NOT do)
  • Keep instructions under 500 tokens for best performance

docs.title: Compilation
docs.text:
const agent = builder.compile();

Compilation validates:

  • All referenced tools exist
  • All journey schemas are valid
  • No circular dependencies between journeys
  • Knowledge sources have valid retrieval functions

docs.title: Custom Events
docs.text:
This guide covers defining and emitting app-specific runtime events.

Work in progress

This guide is being written. Check back soon for complete content.

docs.title: Defining a custom event
docs.text:
import { customRuntimeEvent } from "@cognidesk/core";
import { z } from "zod";

const leadCaptured = customRuntimeEvent("lead.captured", {
  payload: z.object({
    email: z.string().email(),
    source: z.string(),
  }),
  visibleToModel: true,
});

docs.title: Emitting events
docs.text:
await runtime.emitCustomEvent({
  conversationId: conversation.id,
  event: leadCaptured,
  payload: { email: "customer@example.com", source: "pricing-page" },
});

docs.title: Exposing via HTTP
docs.text:
const handler = createCognideskHttpHandler({
  runtime,
  agentId: agent.id,
  basePath: "/api",
  customEvents: [leadCaptured],
});

Custom events are then available via the HTTP API and SSE streaming.

docs.title: HTTP Transport
docs.text:
This guide covers exposing your agent over HTTP using the built-in adapter.

Work in progress

This guide is being written. Check back soon for complete content.

docs.title: Setting up the HTTP handler
docs.text:
import { createCognideskHttpHandler } from "@cognidesk/http";

const handler = createCognideskHttpHandler({
  runtime,
  agentId: agent.id,
  basePath: "/api",
});

docs.title: Endpoints
docs.text:
The HTTP adapter exposes:

Method Path Purpose POST /api/conversations Create a conversation POST /api/conversations/:id/messages Send a message GET /api/conversations/:id/events SSE event stream POST /api/conversations/:id/events Emit custom events POST /api/conversations/:id/journey-events Emit journey events

docs.title: Framework integration
docs.text:
The handler is framework-agnostic. Adapt it to your server:

Node.js / ExpressHonoFastify 
app.use("/api", handler.express());

app.route("/api", handler.hono());

fastify.register(handler.fastify(), { prefix: "/api" });

docs.title: Custom events and journey events
docs.text:
Expose custom events and journey events via HTTP:

const handler = createCognideskHttpHandler({
  runtime,
  agentId: agent.id,
  basePath: "/api",
  customEvents: [leadCaptured],
  journeyEvents: [ticketSynced],
});

docs.title: Journeys
docs.text:
This guide covers building state machine journeys for structured conversation flows.

Work in progress

This guide is being written. Check back soon for complete content.

docs.title: Creating a journey
docs.text:
const ticketStatus = agentBuilder.stateMachineJourney("ticket-status", {
  condition: "Customer wants to check ticket status",
  examples: ["What's the status of my booking?", "Can you check ticket ABC123?"],
  context: z.object({
    bookingReference: z.string().optional(),
    ticketStatus: z.string().optional(),
  }),
});

docs.title: Defining states
docs.text:
const states = ticketStatus.defineStates("identify", "lookup", "confirm");

const identify = states.get("identify").collect("bookingReference");
const lookup = states.get("lookup").runTool(findTicket, {
  input: ({ context }) => ({ bookingReference: context.bookingReference ?? "" }),
  assign: { ticketStatus: ({ output }) => output.status },
});

docs.title: Alternate entries
docs.text:
Skip states when context is already available:

ticketStatus.alternateEntry(lookup, {
  description: "Skip identification when reference was reused.",
  priority: 10,
  when: ({ context }) => context.bookingReference !== undefined,
});

docs.title: Guards
docs.text:
Conditionally allow or block journey activation:

guard: ({ app }) =>
  app.authenticated
    ? { allow: true }
    : { allow: false, code: "auth_required", message: "Please log in." },

docs.title: Journey Events
docs.text:
Enable external systems to drive state changes:

const ticketSynced = ticketStatus.event("ticket.synced", {
  payload: z.object({ bookingReference: z.string() }),
  routing: "activeJourneyOnly",
});

lookup.on(ticketSynced).target(lookup);

docs.title: Knowledge Sources
docs.text:
This guide covers integrating retrieval-augmented knowledge into your agent.

Work in progress

This guide is being written. Check back soon for complete content.

docs.title: Defining a knowledge source
docs.text:
import { knowledgeSource } from "@cognidesk/core";
import { z } from "zod";

const productDocs = knowledgeSource("product-docs", {
  query: z.object({ query: z.string(), category: z.string().optional() }),
  metadata: z.object({ source: z.string(), url: z.string().optional() }),
  retrieve: async ({ query }) => {
    const results = await vectorStore.search(query.query, {
      filter: query.category ? { category: query.category } : undefined,
      limit: 5,
    });
    return {
      items: results.map((r) => ({
        id: r.id,
        content: r.text,
        metadata: { source: "product-docs", url: r.url },
      })),
    };
  },
});

docs.title: UI integration
docs.text: Knowledge source metadata is available in React UI components, enabling hoverable citations and source links in assistant messages.

docs.text:

docs.title: Model configuration
docs.text:
Cognidesk uses a single model entrypoint that supports multiple providers:

import { createRuntime } from "@cognidesk/core";

const runtime = createRuntime({
  storage,
  agent,
  models: {
    chat: chatModel,
    journeyEmbedding: embeddingModel,
  },
});

docs.title: Supported providers
docs.text: Provider Package Notes OpenAI @cognidesk/model GPT-4o, GPT-4, GPT-3.5 OpenRouter @cognidesk/model Multi-model routing Custom Implement ModelAdapter Any OpenAI-compatible API

docs.title: Model adapter interface
docs.text:
import type { ModelAdapter } from "@cognidesk/core";

const customModel: ModelAdapter = {
  // Implement the model adapter contract
};

docs.title: Journey embedding model
docs.text:
Journey activation uses embeddings to match user intent to journey conditions:

import { buildJourneyIndex } from "@cognidesk/core";

const journeyIndex = await buildJourneyIndex(agent, {
  embeddingModel: models.journeyEmbedding,
});

docs.title: Grafana dashboards
docs.text:
The flight demo includes pre-built Grafana dashboards. Run the OTEL stack:

docker compose -f docker-compose.otel.yml up --build

Open http://localhost:3000 for provisioned Cognidesk dashboards showing:

  • Conversation throughput and latency
  • Journey activation and completion rates

docs.title: React UI
docs.text:
This guide covers integrating Cognidesk into React applications with the chat widget and hooks.

Work in progress

This guide is being written. Check back soon for complete content.

docs.title: Client creation
docs.text:
import { createCognideskClient } from "@cognidesk/react";

const client = createCognideskClient({
  baseUrl: "/api",
});

docs.title: Chat widget
docs.text:
Drop in a full chat interface:

import { ChatWidget } from "@cognidesk/react";

function App() {
  return <ChatWidget client={client} agentId="flight-support" />;
}

docs.title: useChat hook
docs.text:
For custom UI, use the lower-level hook:

import { useChat } from "@cognidesk/react";

function CustomChat() {
  const { messages, sendMessage, isStreaming } = useChat({
    client,
    agentId: "flight-support",
  });

  return (
    <div>
      {messages.map((m) => (
        <div key={m.id}>{m.content}</div>
      ))}
    </div>
  );
}

docs.title: Appearance configuration
docs.text:
Style the widget with CSS variables and element classes:

<ChatWidget
  client={client}
  agentId="flight-support"
  appearance={{
    variables: { "--cd-color-primary": "#0f172a" },
    elements: { root: "shadow-lg" },
    widgets: {
      confirmation: {
        elements: {
          panel: "rounded-md border p-3",
          primaryButton: "bg-emerald-700",
        },
      },
    },
  }}
/>

docs.title: Custom widgets
docs.text:
Replace built-in widget kinds with custom React components:

<ChatWidget
  client={client}
  agentId="flight-support"
  widgets={{
    "seat-map": ({ input, submit }) => (
      <SeatMap value={input} onSelect={(seatId) => submit({ seatId })} />
    ),
  }}
/>

docs.title: SSE Streaming
docs.text:
This guide covers real-time event streaming to clients.

Work in progress

This guide is being written. Check back soon for complete content.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Cognidesk uses Server-Sent Events (SSE) to push real-time updates to clients. This enables:

  • Streaming assistant responses (token by token)
  • Journey state change notifications
  • Tool execution progress
  • Custom event delivery

docs.title: Client connection
docs.text:
const eventSource = new EventSource("/api/conversations/123/events");

eventSource.onmessage = (event) => {
  const data = JSON.parse(event.data);
  // Handle event
};

docs.title: Event types
docs.text: Event When message.delta Streaming text tokens message.complete Final message with citations journey.stateChanged Journey state transition tool.executing Tool invocation started tool.complete Tool execution finished

docs.title: Synthetic deltas
docs.text:
Enable token-by-token streaming even when the model returns full responses:

const runtime = createRuntime({
  // ...
  streaming: { syntheticDeltas: true },
});

docs.title: Storage
docs.text:
This guide covers configuring conversation persistence.

Work in progress

This guide is being written. Check back soon for complete content.

docs.title: SQLite storage
docs.text:
The built-in SQLite adapter provides local conversation persistence:

pnpm add @cognidesk/storage @libsql/client

import { createSqliteStorage } from "@cognidesk/storage/sqlite";

const storage = createSqliteStorage({
  filename: "conversations.sqlite",
});

const runtime = createRuntime({ storage, agent, models });

docs.title: Postgres storage
docs.text:
Use Postgres for production deployments that need a server database:

pnpm add @cognidesk/storage pg

import { createPostgresStorage } from "@cognidesk/storage/postgres";

const storage = createPostgresStorage({
  url: process.env.DATABASE_URL!,
});

const runtime = createRuntime({ storage, agent, models });

docs.title: Storage contract
docs.text:
The storage interface is defined in @cognidesk/core. Any implementation that satisfies the contract can be used:

  • Conversation records
  • Runtime Event storage and replay
  • Runtime Snapshot persistence
  • Conversation lifecycle updates

docs.title: Custom storage adapters
docs.text:
Implement the storage interface to use your own database:

import type { StorageAdapter } from "@cognidesk/core";

const customStorage: StorageAdapter = {
  // Implement the storage contract
};

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
This guide covers defining, registering, and using tools in your agent.

Work in progress

This guide is being written. Check back soon for complete content.

docs.title: Defining a tool
docs.text:
import { tool } from "@cognidesk/core";
import { z } from "zod";

const cancelBooking = tool("cancelBooking", {
  input: z.object({
    bookingReference: z.string().describe("Booking reference to cancel"),
    reason: z.enum(["customer_request", "duplicate", "fraud"]),
  }),
  output: z.object({
    success: z.boolean(),
    refundAmount: z.number().optional(),
  }),
  execute: async ({ input }) => {
    const result = await bookingService.cancel(input.bookingReference, input.reason);
    return { success: result.ok, refundAmount: result.refund };
  },
});

docs.title: Registering tools
docs.text:
const builder = createAgent("support", { instructions: "..." });
builder.tools.add(cancelBooking);
builder.tools.add(findTicket);
builder.tools.add(searchFlights);

docs.title: Tools in journey states
docs.text:
Tools can be bound to specific journey states for deterministic execution:

const lookup = states.get("lookup").runTool(findTicket, {
  input: ({ context }) => ({ bookingReference: context.bookingReference ?? "" }),
  assign: { ticketStatus: ({ output }) => output.status },
});

docs.title: Error handling
docs.text: Tool execution errors are captured and surfaced to the model as structured error responses, allowing it to recover gracefully.

docs.title: Voice
docs.text:
This guide covers real-time voice conversations with Cognidesk.

Work in progress

This guide is being written. Check back soon for complete content.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Cognidesk supports live voice conversations through pluggable Voice Connection Adapters. Voice shares the same runtime definitions (agents, journeys, tools, knowledge) as text chat.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Browser] -->|Voice Browser Protocol| B[Voice WebSocket Adapter]
    B --> C[Voice Connection Adapter]
    C --> D[OpenAI Realtime API]
    C --> E[Cognidesk Runtime]

docs.title: Voice profiles
docs.text:
Configure voice as an agent profile:

const agent = createAgent("support", {
  instructions: "...",
  voiceProfile: {
    voice: "alloy",
    instructions: "Keep responses under 30 seconds. Be conversational.",
  },
});

docs.title: Key concepts
docs.text:
• Voice Browser Protocol — Cognidesk-owned WebSocket protocol for browser voice
  • Voice Audio Deltas — Base64-encoded PCM audio (16-bit, mono, 24 kHz)
  • Server-Mediated Connection — Provider sessions managed server-side
  • Voice Events — Durable runtime events from voice interactions

docs.title: Shared definitions
docs.text: Voice conversations use the same journeys, tools, and knowledge as text — no duplication needed. The runtime automatically optimizes definitions for voice context.
